title: The Romans of Partenay
summary: CME00045 is the General Corpus XML representation of the anonymous Middle English rhyme-royal Romans of Partenay. The repository file copies the University of Michigan encoding, which selects Skeat's revised 1899 EETS text. Skeat's edition transcribes the unique English witness, Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17, while consulting Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5, a French Coudrette manuscript, for punctuation, interpretation, and the passage lost with R.3.17 folio 88. This graph distinguishes work, witnesses, editions, encoding, repository artifact, and digital surrogates. Complete publicly exposed objects are cached for R.3.17 and both Skeat editions; only a selected f.1r image was publicly found for Ll.2.5, and the dated whole-object negative is explicit.
review_notes: The cataloging subject is the anonymous Middle English translation/adaptation, not Coudrette's French source, either manuscript, Skeat's edition, or the U-M encoding.
The 57 French lines and 57 modern English editorial lines printed for the lost folio 88 passage are edition-level supply streams, not languages of the surviving Middle English work.
Skeat's repeated consultation of Ll.2.5 establishes an editorial comparison source, not the physical exemplar used by the medieval translator.

The complete R.3.17 cache contains all 258 canvases exposed by Trinity, from front cover through back cover; the work occupies f.2r–124v, canvases 9–252. The provider exposes no separate spine or edge photographs.
Textual lineation, manuscript foliation, printed pagination, PDF pages, and IIIF canvas positions are kept as separate locator systems.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00045
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by the repository publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00045.
TEMPHEAD records a last review date of 2016-08-19.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00045
entities.description: Public electronic text titled The Romans of Partenay, encoding the 1899 title page and edited text on printed pages 1–226, including Skeat's parallel French and modern English supply for missing folio 88 but excluding the edition's prefatory and back matter.

entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, The Romans of Partenay, revised 1899 edition
alternate_labels: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine
entities.description: Skeat's EETS edition first published in 1866 and revised and reprinted in 1899. It presents a near-facsimile transcription of R.3.17, extensive editorial punctuation and apparatus, and a bilingual editorial replacement for the leaf missing after line 4550.

bibliographic.citation: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine, translated from the French of La Coudrette (about 1500–1520 A.D.), edited from a unique manuscript in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, with an introduction, notes, and glossarial index, by Walter W. Skeat, EETS Original Series 22 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1866; revised and reprinted 1899).
bibliographic.title: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 22
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

notes: The title page retains 1866 and adds '[Revised and reprinted 1899.]'.
The exact inventory of changes from 1866 to 1899 was not established; the volume itself contains explicit 1899 additions.

entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, The Romans of Partenay, first edition (1866)
entities.description: The first issue of Skeat's EETS transcription and apparatus, later revised and reprinted in 1899.

bibliographic.citation: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine, edited by Walter W. Skeat, EETS Original Series 22 (London: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1866).
bibliographic.title: The Romans of Partenay or of Lusignen, otherwise known as The Tale of Melusine
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 22
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.17
alternate_labels: The Romance of Earl Raymond of Poitiers in English
entities.description: The unique surviving manuscript witness of the Middle English Romans of Partenay. The fifteenth-century paper codex is imperfect at the beginning because f.1 is wanting and has a second lacuna where f.88 is wanting; the surviving poem runs from f.2r through f.124v.

physical_description.extent: 123 folios; f.1 and f.88 are wanting
notes: The catalog reports four seven-line stanzas to a page.
The surviving text begins imperfectly on f.2r and ends on f.124v.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5
entities.description: A fifteenth-century French paper manuscript containing Coudrette's Roman de Mélusine or Roman de Parthenay on fols. 1–148, followed by an anonymous French chronicle on fols. 149r–161v. Skeat used it constantly as a comparison manuscript and took the French replacement for R.3.17's missing folio 88 from Ll.2.5 f.103v onward.

holding.notes: From the library of John Moore; acquired by Cambridge University Library in 1715.

physical_description.extent: At least 161 folios; Coudrette text on fols. 1–148
notes: The exact endpoint and quire-level extent should be checked against a full institutional catalog record before treating the Biblissima summary as a complete codicological description.

entities.label: Trinity College complete IIIF sequence for MS R.3.17
entities.description: The complete 258-canvas object exposed by Trinity's IIIF manifest, from front cover through back cover, cached at the requested 1800-pixel-wide profile.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and JPEG page images
notes: The work occupies f.2r–124v, canvases 9–252; the cache deliberately retains covers, flyleaves, and all other provider-exposed matter.

entities.label: Bayerische Staatsbibliothek digital facsimile of Skeat's revised 1899 edition
entities.description: A complete 318-canvas IIIF sequence of the BSB copy, shelfmark P.o.angl. 313 ra-22. The U-M-selected text lies on printed pages 1–226, provider canvases 27–252.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and JPEG page images

entities.label: Internet Archive/Google scan of Skeat's 1866 first edition
entities.description: A complete 323-page PDF scan of the first edition, digitized from a Harvard copy by Google and distributed through Internet Archive.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF and Internet Archive item metadata

entities.label: Cambridge University Library selected image of Ll.2.5 f.1r
entities.description: A single image published in a CUL Special Collections blog post. It is partial evidence only and does not constitute a complete public manuscript facsimile.
entities.reproduction_format: PNG image served from a .jpg URL

entities.label: Jenni Nuttall, 'Margaret of Anjou and The Romans of Partenay' (2016)
entities.description: Peer-reviewed scholarship on the Middle English translation's form, possible context, translator, and relationship to Liber Proverbiorum. The author argues that shared form and diction make common translation authorship highly likely while treating Margaret of Anjou as a plausible but unprovable patron.

bibliographic.citation: Jenni Nuttall, 'Margaret of Anjou and The Romans of Partenay', Review of English Studies 67 (2016).
bibliographic.title: Margaret of Anjou and The Romans of Partenay

scope.notes: Preface, contents, notes, glossary, index, and other front/back matter were not encoded.
The title page asserts the relationship; an exhaustive correction/reset inventory remains open.

assertion.notes: Skeat says the proof sheets were compared twice with the manuscript.

locators: f.103v onward for the supplied lacuna

scope.notes: Skeat used Ll.2.5 for punctuation and explanation throughout and printed French from it where R.3.17 f.88 is missing.

locators: f.103v onward for the supplied lacuna

access.access_method: Open the manuscript catalog viewer or its complete IIIF Presentation manifest.

local_copies.label: Complete Trinity IIIF sequence for MS R.3.17

work_portion.label: The Romans of Partenay in the complete manuscript object
locators: Universal Viewer zero-based canvas values cv=8 through cv=251
notes: The first missing leaf, f.1, is not represented by a text image; f.88 is likewise physically absent.
Complete means all provider canvases from front-cover through back-cover, including inside covers and flyleaves.
Trinity exposes no separate spine or edge photographs.
All 258 requests use the exact IIIF width syntax 1800,; sample server responses are 1801 × 2446 pixels because of provider-side rounding, with no local upscaling.
Archive integrity, the 258-image count, and boundaries front-cover / f002r / f124v / back-cover were independently verified.

notes: Public image availability does not itself answer whether the noncommercially licensed photographs may be republished in a repository that supports commercial print-on-demand use.

notes: Only f.1r is represented. This image cannot satisfy the whole-manuscript caching policy and is retained as explicit partial evidence.
No work_portion is asserted because this is a leaf of the French comparison witness, not an image of the Middle English CME00045 text.

access.access_method: No complete manuscript object was located in CUL Digital Library, ArchiveSearch, the Biblissima record, or the institutional blog as checked on the stated date.
notes: Generic Mirador controls on an aggregation page are not evidence that a complete provider image sequence exists.

access.access_method: Request new or existing images through Cambridge University Library Imaging Services.
access.format: Digital image order subject to quotation and permissions
notes: The published minimum charge is £25 excluding VAT; a whole 161-folio manuscript order could be substantially more expensive and requires a quotation.

rights.component: Fifteenth-century Middle English text as an underlying historical work
rights.basis: The text predates modern copyright, and U-M marks the source materials public domain. This does not determine rights in modern photographs, editions, transcriptions, or platform files.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and displayed source materials
rights.basis: The U-M item labels the materials public domain and links the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.

rights.component: 1899 editorial text, typography, and underlying medieval texts
rights.basis: The 1899 publication is beyond the ordinary U.S. copyright term; this conclusion is separate from provider conditions on a particular scan.

rights.component: 1866 editorial text, typography, and underlying medieval texts
rights.basis: The 1866 publication is beyond the ordinary U.S. copyright term; Internet Archive metadata also reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the U.S.

rights.component: BSB/Google digital images and supplied files
rights.jurisdiction: Germany and provider terms; copyright status may differ elsewhere
rights.basis: BSB applies the No Copyright–Non-Commercial Use Only 1.0 statement. Its FAQ permits noncommercial use and limits commercial publication of Google-digitized material to uses where those scans are not predominant or sole.
notes: A repository copy of the complete scan would make the provider images predominant or sole, so the consulted terms do not support that commercial/POD redistribution.
BSB requests attribution including library, shelfmark, page or folio, URN, and 'digitized by Google'.

rights.jurisdiction: United States; provider notices may have broader application
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and U.S. as the copyright region. The embedded Google notice separately requests personal noncommercial use, attribution, retention of watermarks, and no automated querying.
notes: Public-domain status of the underlying edition and provider-requested conditions for this particular scan are recorded separately.

rights.component: Trinity College manuscript photographs and IIIF image sequence
rights.basis: The item manifest applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0. Trinity's Wren Digital Library policy separately directs users to request permission for publication and says fees depend on use.
notes: The user's repository enables commercial and noncommercial print-on-demand publication. Republishing these photographs there therefore requires express review or permission even though the underlying medieval text is public domain.

rights.component: Cambridge University Library photograph of Ll.2.5 f.1r and any newly ordered images
rights.basis: No image-specific open license was found. CUL's imaging terms frame supplied copies as private/noncommercial research copies and require written permission or a license for publication, online use, or commercial use.
notes: The cached f.1r image is research evidence only and is not cleared for repository redistribution or commercial/POD use.

rights.basis: The accepted manuscript identifies Jenni Nuttall as copyright holder. ORA states that authors retain copyright and permits one download/print copy for private noncommercial study unless item-specific terms say otherwise, excluding profit-making use.
notes: The local PDF supports research and verification; it is not cleared for public repository redistribution.

editorial_practices.representation: Title page and edited text on printed pp. 1–226
editorial_practices.description: U-M encodes the title page and edited poem, including Skeat's folio 88 replacement, but omits the edition's preface, contents, notes, glossary, index, and other front/back matter.
Skeat expands manuscript contractions and distinguishes the supplied letters typographically with italics. These italics therefore carry documentary information rather than ordinary emphasis.
The edition represents red manuscript stanza initials by enlarged printed letters rather than reproducing their color.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Sparse manuscript punctuation and line-end marks
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial punctuation, parentheses, quotation marks, and omitted routine full points/strokes
editorial_practices.description: Skeat supplies punctuation and interpretive grouping, omits line-end full points and routine strokes or tags, and says Ll.2.5 was consulted constantly for punctuation and explanation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Long s, metrical downstrokes, manuscript spacing, later marginal notes, and absent letters
editorial_practices.representation: Modern s; omitted downstrokes; hyphens for intra-word manuscript spaces; marginalia moved to footnotes; square brackets for editorial supply
editorial_practices.description: The edition calls itself near-facsimile but uses explicit conventions instead of reproducing every graphic feature. It preserves some documentary distinctions while normalizing or relocating others.

editorial_practices.source_feature: French comparison manuscript Ll.2.5 and missing R.3.17 f.88
editorial_practices.representation: 57 French source lines and 57 modern English editorial lines printed in parallel streams on pp. 158–159
editorial_practices.description: After Middle English line 4550, Skeat prints French beginning at Ll.2.5 f.103v and an editorial modern English rendering for the passage missing with R.3.17 f.88. The main Middle English numbering resumes at 4607, implying 56 missing numbered Middle English lines even though each supplied editorial stream contains 57 physical lines.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed pp. 158–159; XML DIV3 language streams fre and eng
notes: The two supply streams duplicate printed page markers 158 and 159 in XML; page-break element counts must therefore use unique references when answering page-coverage questions.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00045.xml
evidence.locator: IDG; title page; BODY/DIV1; Middle English, French, and modern English line streams; notes and page breaks
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for repository identity, inherited bibliographic description, textual structure, lineation, page references, editorial supply streams, and notes.

evidence.citation: Local structural and checksum inspection of CME00045.xml
evidence.locator: Element, language, line-number, stanza, note, and unique page-reference counts
evidence.summary: Confirmed 6,883 L elements: 6,769 Middle English, 57 French, and 57 modern English editorial lines; 969 LG elements: 967 Middle English and two supply groups; 229 PB elements but 227 unique REF values and 226 unique printed N values; 2,220 notes; 210 surviving prologue lines; poem lineation 1–6615 with lines 4551–4606 absent; and line ranges 6434–6545 for the litany and 6546–6615 for the translator's epilogue.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English, CME00045 item record
evidence.summary: Identifies the item, cites Skeat's EETS edition, exposes the electronic text, and supplies the stable U-M identifier.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English about page
evidence.summary: Describes the 2016–2018 expansion as manually keyed from public-domain editions with in-house proofreading and basic TEI, but does not establish item-specific production history for CME00045.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English, CME00045 rights statement
evidence.summary: Marks the materials public domain and links No Copyright–United States 1.0.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Romans of Partenay, EETS OS 22, revised and reprinted 1899, BSB digital object bsb12036890
evidence.locator: Title page, preface, edited pp. 1–226, notes, glossary, and index
evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence for title, bibliographic history, manuscript identification, transcription conventions, comparison against Ll.2.5, missing-folio supply, and the U-M-selected page range.

evidence.citation: Bayerische Staatsbibliothek IIIF Presentation manifest for bsb12036890
evidence.locator: 318 canvases; title page canvas 9; printed pp. 1–226 canvases 27–252
evidence.summary: Defines the complete public image sequence, labels, image-service URLs, identifiers, and item-level rights statement used for caching and work-portion mapping.

evidence.citation: Bayerische Staatsbibliothek Digital Collections FAQ and bsb12036890 item rights statement
evidence.summary: States the NoC-NC status, noncommercial permission, attribution requirements, and the condition that Google scans may be used commercially only when they are not predominant or sole.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Romans of Partenay, EETS OS 22 (1866), Internet Archive item romanspartenayo01skeagoog
evidence.summary: Primary digital evidence for the first edition and the embedded Google scan notice.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 52887, The Romans of Partenay (1866)
evidence.summary: Confirms the first edition's 1866 N. Trübner publication and xix, 299-page collation, correcting misleading later-publisher metadata in the Internet Archive record.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for romanspartenayo01skeagoog
evidence.summary: Identifies the Google/Harvard scan, 323-image extent, digitization/publication dates, NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT status, and U.S. copyright region; its bibliographic publisher/date fields are not used against the scanned title page and Folger record.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive scan-data XML for romanspartenayo01skeagoog
evidence.summary: Records the provider's 323-page scan structure and page metadata.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, online manuscript catalog, MS R.3.17
evidence.summary: Identifies the unique fifteenth-century paper witness, alternate title, 123-folio extent, four rhyme-royal stanzas per page, missing fols. 1 and 88, and surviving text from f.2r to f.124v.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge IIIF Presentation manifest for MS R.3.17
evidence.locator: 258 canvases; front-cover to back-cover; f.2r canvas 9; f.124v canvas 252
evidence.summary: Defines the complete public object, image services, canvas labels, exact work portion, and CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, Wren Digital Library reproduction and publication guidance
evidence.summary: Permits image downloads for personal study and directs publication/reuse permission requests to the Wren Library; fees and permission depend on intended use. The item manifest separately supplies CC BY-NC 4.0.

evidence.citation: Biblissima+ authority aggregation for Cambridge University Library MS Ll.2.5
evidence.summary: Identifies the fifteenth-century manuscript, Coudrette romance on fols. 1–148, and following anonymous French chronicle on fols. 149r–161v. It does not establish a complete public image sequence.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library Special Collections blog post featuring MS Ll.2.5
evidence.summary: Describes Ll.2.5 as a fifteenth-century paper manuscript from John Moore's library, acquired in 1715, and supplies a selected f.1r image only.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library selected image of MS Ll.2.5 f.1r
evidence.summary: A single institutional image, cached as explicit partial evidence; the response is image/png despite the .jpg URL suffix.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, ordering images and Imaging Services portal
evidence.summary: Provides the image-order route, contact, minimum £25 charge excluding VAT, research-copy restrictions, and publication/online/commercial permission requirements.

evidence.citation: Jenni Nuttall, 'Margaret of Anjou and The Romans of Partenay', Review of English Studies 67 (2016), accepted manuscript in Oxford ORA
evidence.summary: Supports the mid-fifteenth-century literary context, rhyme-royal form, the translator's non-native French, likely common authorship with Liber Proverbiorum, translator epilogue lines 6546–6615, and the careful distinction between plausible and provable patronage.

evidence.citation: Oxford University Research Archive terms of use
evidence.summary: States that authors retain copyright and permits one copy for private noncommercial study unless otherwise specified, excluding profit-making use.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, 'The Romans of Partenay'
evidence.summary: Supplies discovery metadata including c.1500 dating, tentative Northeast Midlands association, rhyme royal form, IMEV 819.5, Coudrette source, unique R.3.17 witness and folio range, and 6,615-line main-poem extent.

open_questions.question: Exactly which readings, apparatus entries, pagination features, and typographic details changed between Skeat's 1866 first edition and the revised 1899 reprint?
next_steps: Perform a structured page- and text-level comparison of the two complete cached volumes.

open_questions.question: Does CUL hold an unindexed complete digital surrogate of MS Ll.2.5, or what would a complete new digital reproduction cost and permit?
next_steps: Ask CUL Imaging Services whether a complete existing surrogate is available.
Request a whole-object quotation and written reuse terms before ordering.
notes: The dated negative is whole-object specific; one selected f.1r image is public and cached but is not treated as a manuscript facsimile.

open_questions.question: Can a specific French textual ancestor or manuscript relation between Ll.2.5 and the medieval translator's exemplar be established?
next_steps: Consult a modern critical study of the Coudrette manuscript tradition and the English translation's source readings.
notes: Skeat's use of Ll.2.5 as his editorial comparison manuscript must not be promoted into evidence that it was the translator's physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: Why do Skeat's French and modern English supply streams each contain 57 physical lines while the Middle English numbering leaves a 56-line gap from 4551 through 4606?
next_steps: Align the 57 French and 57 English editorial lines clause by clause against the surrounding Middle English and Ll.2.5 f.103v onward.

open_questions.question: Was CME00045 manually keyed during the 2016–2018 expansion, converted from an earlier digital transcription, or produced by another item-specific workflow?
next_steps: Seek item-level production documentation or U-M staff confirmation.
notes: The collection-level about page does not prove an item-specific workflow.
